molecular_profile: Occupational etiology: hardwood dust exposure (furniture maker)

treatments.name: Endoscopic Endonasal Resection + Adjuvant IMRT (60-66 Gy)

evidence_level.rationale: Large retrospective cohort of 200 patients (Frontiers in Oncology 2025) with long-term follow-up. No Phase 3 RCTs exist for this rare tumor but strong institutional series consistently demonstrate outcomes. EEA has become standard-of-reference for most sinonasal malignancies

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 71.4%, 5-year DSS 85.1% in 200-patient series. EEA correlated with improved DSS vs external surgery for T3-T4 tumors. For T3N0M0 with clear margins, outcomes approach 70-80% 5-year survival

accessibility.rationale: Available at major academic medical centers with experienced skull base surgical teams. Requires multidisciplinary team (ENT, neurosurgery, radiation oncology). Not available at all community hospitals

safety_profile.rationale: EEA has fewer complications than open craniofacial resection: shorter hospitalization (4 vs 7 days), improved cosmetic outcome, no facial incisions. Main risks include CSF leak (5-10%), epistaxis, orbital complications

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Universal applicability for all ITAC subtypes regardless of molecular profile. Surgery is the cornerstone for all resectable disease

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Endoscopic endonasal approach provides minimally invasive transnasal access for complete tumor resection with transnasal craniectomy for cribriform plate involvement. Adjuvant IMRT delivers conformal radiation (60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions) to the tumor bed and at-risk margins while sparing critical structures (optic nerves, brain). Combined modality targets residual microscopic disease and reduces local recurrence from 50% (surgery alone) to approximately 15-20%.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-term outcomes of endoscopic resection and tailored adjuvant radiotherapy for sinonasal ITAC (200-patient cohort)

notable_side_effects: Late RT toxicity: osteoradionecrosis of skull base (rare)
treatments.availability: Available at major academic skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Topical 5-Fluorouracil (Post-Surgical Adjuvant)

evidence_level.rationale: 20-year case-controlled study demonstrating statistically significant improvement in disease-free survival (86% vs 50% with RT alone, p=0.03). Multiple institutional series confirm efficacy. Originally described for woodworkers' adenocarcinoma specifically

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year DFS improved from 50% to 86% (p=0.03). Earliest series showed 100% 5-year survival with adjuvant low-dose RT + topical 5-FU. Directly addresses the high local recurrence rate (34-50%) that defines ITAC biology

accessibility.rationale: 5-FU topical formulation is inexpensive and widely available. Administration is straightforward: once or twice weekly for 4-6 weeks post-operatively via endoscopic application to the surgical cavity

safety_profile.rationale: Minimal systemic absorption with topical application. Local side effects limited to nasal irritation, crusting, and mild mucosal inflammation. No systemic chemotherapy toxicity. Well-tolerated in outpatient setting

biomarker_match.rationale: Exploits intestinal phenotype of ITAC (CDX2+, CK20+) which confers 5-FU sensitivity analogous to colorectal cancer. Particularly relevant for this woodworker population where ITAC was first described

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Topical 5-FU applied directly to the post-surgical nasal cavity delivers high local drug concentrations to the resection margins where microscopic residual disease and early recurrence are most likely. 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase in residual tumor cells, blocking DNA synthesis. The intestinal differentiation of ITAC (CDX2+/CK20+) recapitulates colorectal cancer biology, providing biological rationale for fluoropyrimidine sensitivity. Applied 1-2x weekly for 4-6 weeks post-surgery.

key_evidence.study_name: Is surgical debridement and topical 5-fluorouracil the optimum treatment for woodworkers' adenocarcinoma of the ethmoid sinuses? A case-controlled study of a 20-year experience

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (5-FU topical) — off-label for sinonasal application

treatments.name: Endoscopic Debulking + Topical 5-FU (Without Formal Radiation — Recurrence Management)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple institutional series over 20+ years. JAMA Otolaryngology publication demonstrated this approach as alternative to craniofacial resection with comparable survival. 5-year DFS 86% with surgery + topical 5-FU. Particularly validated for woodworkers' adenocarcinoma

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year DFS 86% in surgery + topical 5-FU group. Comparable oncologic outcomes to more aggressive approaches. Advantages for recurrence management where re-irradiation may be contraindicated

accessibility.rationale: Simple outpatient procedure combining endoscopic debulking with topical 5-FU application. Minimal resource requirements. Can be repeated for multiple recurrences

safety_profile.rationale: Minimal morbidity: outpatient procedure, no systemic toxicity from topical application, no radiation side effects. Can be repeated multiple times without cumulative toxicity

biomarker_match.rationale: CDX2+/CK20+ intestinal phenotype supports fluoropyrimidine sensitivity. Originally described specifically for woodworkers' ethmoid adenocarcinoma — directly applicable to this patient's occupational etiology

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines endoscopic surgical debulking to remove macroscopic disease with direct topical application of 5-fluorouracil to the surgical cavity. Local 5-FU delivery achieves high drug concentrations at the mucosal surface where recurrence originates, without systemic exposure. This approach is particularly suited for managing local recurrences when formal radiation has already been delivered and re-irradiation carries excessive toxicity risk.

key_evidence.study_name: Adenocarcinoma of the Ethmoidal Sinus Complex: Surgical Debulking and Topical Fluorouracil May Be the Optimal Treatment

treatments.availability: Widely available — standard ENT procedure with generic 5-FU

treatments.name: Open Craniofacial Resection + Adjuvant IMRT

evidence_level.rationale: Historical gold standard with decades of institutional data from major centers. Multiple retrospective series (95-patient series, Annals of Surgical Oncology 2010) demonstrate long-term efficacy. Still considered for cases with extensive dural/intracranial invasion not amenable to EEA

survival_benefit.rationale: Disease-specific 5-year survival 78% in 95-patient series. Comparable oncologic outcomes to EEA in most studies but historically higher morbidity. For T3 cribriform plate involvement, provides maximal surgical access

accessibility.rationale: Available at most academic medical centers with head and neck surgical oncology programs. Does not require the specialized endoscopic skull base expertise that EEA demands

safety_profile.rationale: Higher morbidity than EEA: longer hospitalization (7+ days vs 4), facial incisions, frontal craniotomy risks, higher CSF leak rate, wound complications, potential neurocognitive effects. Mortality rate 2-5% in historical series

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Universal applicability for all ITAC histological subtypes

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Open transfacial/craniofacial approach combines lateral rhinotomy or midfacial degloving with bifrontal craniotomy for en bloc resection of tumor involving the ethmoid sinuses, cribriform plate, and adjacent skull base. Provides wide surgical exposure for complete resection with direct visualization. Adjuvant IMRT targets the resection cavity to reduce local recurrence.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-Term Single-Center Results of Management of Ethmoid Adenocarcinoma: 95 Patients over 28 Years

treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers with skull base surgery programs

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant or Definitive)

evidence_level.rationale: Large retrospective series (143 patients, PMC 2021) with 4.9-year median follow-up for survivors. NCCN guidelines support proton therapy for sinonasal tumors at the skull base. No randomized comparison to IMRT exists

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 59%, 5-year local control 80% in mixed sinonasal histology cohort. For gross total resection subgroup, 5-year local control reaches 87%. Superior dose conformality at skull base may reduce late toxicity without sacrificing tumor control

accessibility.rationale: Limited to specialized proton therapy centers (approximately 40 in the US, fewer globally). Significantly more expensive than IMRT. May require travel and relocation during treatment. Insurance coverage variable

safety_profile.rationale: Superior dose localization reduces radiation to critical structures (optic nerves, brain, brainstem). Grade 3+ late toxicity 22%. Unilateral vision loss 3.5%. CNS necrosis 6%. Lower expected rates of long-term neurocognitive effects than photon therapy

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Benefits all sinonasal tumor histologies equally. Particularly advantageous for skull base tumors near critical structures regardless of molecular profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit their maximum energy at a precise depth (Bragg peak) with minimal exit dose beyond the tumor target. This allows delivery of high doses (73.6 GyRBE median) to ethmoid/skull base tumors while dramatically reducing dose to the optic nerves, chiasm, temporal lobes, and brainstem compared to photon-based IMRT. Particularly advantageous for T3 tumors with cribriform plate involvement where critical neural structures are immediately adjacent.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-term Outcomes from Proton Therapy for Sinonasal Cancers
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

treatments.availability: Available at specialized proton therapy centers — NCCN-supported for skull base tumors

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Cisplatin + 5-FU + Leucovorin (PFL) Followed by Surgery + RT

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial data with 30 patients showing 40% pathologic complete response rate. TP53 status strongly predicts response (83% pCR in TP53 wild-type vs 11% in mutant). No Phase 3 RCTs. Limited by small sample sizes inherent to rare tumor

survival_benefit.rationale: 40% pathological complete response rate suggests potential for improved outcomes. May downstage tumors to enable less extensive surgery. Rationale strong given ITAC intestinal biology with shared CK20/CDX2 expression paralleling colorectal cancer

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin, 5-FU, and leucovorin are widely available generic chemotherapy agents. Can be administered at any oncology center with infusion capabilities

safety_profile.rationale: Standard platinum-based chemotherapy toxicity: nephrotoxicity, myelosuppression, mucositis, nausea/vomiting. May delay surgery if significant toxicity occurs. Requires adequate renal function and performance status

biomarker_match.rationale: Response strongly predicted by TP53 status (83% pCR in wild-type). CDX2+ and CK20+ intestinal phenotype provides biological rationale for fluoropyrimidine-based therapy. Should assess TP53 status before treatment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin creates DNA crosslinks and adducts causing cell death. 5-Fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase, blocking DNA synthesis — exploiting the intestinal differentiation biology of ITAC (shared with colorectal cancer sensitivity to fluoropyrimidines). Leucovorin enhances 5-FU cytotoxicity by stabilizing the FdUMP-thymidylate synthase complex. Neoadjuvant use aims to shrink tumor, achieve pathologic response, and potentially enable less morbid surgery.

key_evidence.study_name: Tp53 status as guide for the management of ethmoid sinus intestinal-type adenocarcinoma
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Head & Neck

biomarker_requirements: TP53 wild-type status strongly predicts response (83% pCR vs 11% if mutant)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (agents individually) — off-label combination for ITAC

treatments.name: FOLFOX (5-FU + Oxaliplatin) for Advanced/Recurrent ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: Small retrospective series of 6 patients with advanced ITAC showing 100% response rate. No controlled trials. Rationale derived from established FOLFOX efficacy in colorectal cancer with analogous biology (CDX2+, CK20+, intestinal differentiation)

survival_benefit.rationale: All 6 patients achieved response including those with brain extension. Median PFS 6.0 months. Meaningful clinical responses in a disease with no standard systemic therapy for advanced cases. Limited by small sample size

accessibility.rationale: FOLFOX is a standard colorectal cancer regimen available at all oncology centers worldwide. Components (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin) are generic and widely accessible

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile from extensive colorectal cancer experience. Peripheral neuropathy (oxaliplatin-specific), myelosuppression, diarrhea, mucositis. Toxicity profile was described as usual with no unexpected adverse events in the ITAC series

biomarker_match.rationale: Strong biological rationale: ITAC shares CK20+, CDX2+, villin+, MUC2+ immunophenotype with colorectal adenocarcinoma. This patient's intestinal marker profile directly supports fluoropyrimidine-based combination therapy

treatments.mechanism_of_action: FOLFOX exploits the molecular similarity between ITAC and colorectal adenocarcinoma. 5-Fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase (DNA synthesis blockade), leucovorin potentiates 5-FU activity, and oxaliplatin forms platinum-DNA adducts causing inter- and intra-strand crosslinks. The intestinal differentiation of ITAC (CDX2+, CK20+, villin+) recapitulates the biology of colorectal cancer, providing rational basis for colorectal chemotherapy regimens.

key_evidence.study_name: 5-fluorouracil with oxaliplatin and/or irinotecan for advanced sinonasal intestinal-type adenocarcinoma

biomarker_requirements: CDX2+ and CK20+ intestinal immunophenotype supports rationale
KRAS/BRAF wild-type status may predict better response
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for colorectal cancer — off-label for ITAC

treatments.name: FOLFIRI (5-FU + Irinotecan) for Advanced/Recurrent ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: Same retrospective series as FOLFOX (6 patients total, 2 received FOLFIRI). Responses observed including in patients with brain extension. No controlled trials. Biological rationale identical to FOLFOX

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 5.8 months, comparable to FOLFOX. Alternative option for patients who cannot tolerate oxaliplatin-related neuropathy. All patients achieved response in the series

accessibility.rationale: FOLFIRI is a standard colorectal cancer regimen available globally. All components are generic and widely accessible

safety_profile.rationale: Avoids oxaliplatin neuropathy but has irinotecan-specific toxicity: delayed diarrhea (potentially severe), cholinergic syndrome. Well-characterized from extensive colorectal cancer experience

biomarker_match.rationale: Same intestinal biology rationale as FOLFOX. CDX2+/CK20+ phenotype supports fluoropyrimidine sensitivity. May consider UGT1A1 genotyping for irinotecan dose adjustment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines 5-FU/leucovorin (thymidylate synthase inhibition) with irinotecan (topoisomerase I inhibitor causing DNA double-strand breaks). Exploits the same intestinal differentiation biology of ITAC that makes it sensitive to colorectal cancer regimens. Irinotecan provides a distinct mechanism of action from oxaliplatin, offering an alternative for neuropathy-intolerant patients.

key_evidence.study_name: 5-fluorouracil with oxaliplatin and/or irinotecan for advanced sinonasal intestinal-type adenocarcinoma

biomarker_requirements: CDX2+ and CK20+ intestinal immunophenotype supports rationale
Consider UGT1A1 genotyping for irinotecan dose adjustment
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for colorectal cancer — off-label for ITAC

treatments.name: Perioperative Pembrolizumab + Surgery + RT (Checkpoint Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: No direct evidence in ITAC. Extrapolated from KEYNOTE-689 Phase 3 trial in locally advanced HNSCC (EFS 59.8% vs 45.9% at 36 months). NCT05925491 is actively evaluating pembrolizumab in locally advanced sinonasal carcinoma but results pending. ITAC has low PD-L1 expression (17% with >5% staining) suggesting limited benefit

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 showed significant EFS improvement in HNSCC. However, ITAC is poorly immunogenic: only 6% exhibit CD8-high/PD-L1-positive phenotype, only 2% are MSI-high. Expected benefit substantially lower than in HNSCC

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved for multiple indications including head and neck cancer. Widely available. Off-label use for ITAC would require clinical justification and possibly insurance appeal

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized immune-related adverse events: thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis. Generally manageable with corticosteroids. Does not impair surgical outcomes based on KEYNOTE-689 data

biomarker_match.rationale: ITAC is poorly immunogenic — only 17% show PD-L1 >5%, only 3% exceed CPS 50, and MSI-high frequency is approximately 2%. Without PD-L1 testing showing high expression, expected benefit is marginal. This patient's intestinal-type tumor biology suggests low immunogenicity

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint interaction, reactivating T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity. Neoadjuvant administration aims to prime immune responses before surgery, while adjuvant dosing maintains immune surveillance against residual disease. However, ITAC typically has low tumor mutational burden and PD-L1 expression, limiting checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-689 (Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer)

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS testing recommended — low expression expected in ITAC
MSI/dMMR testing — approximately 2% of ITAC are MSI-high (FDA-approved indication)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for HNSCC and MSI-high tumors — clinical trial or off-label for ITAC

treatments.name: Cetuximab (Anti-EGFR) for EGFR-Overexpressing Recurrent ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: Single case report only (PMC 2019). EGFR overexpression present in 63% of ITAC but no clinical trials evaluating anti-EGFR therapy specifically. The sole reported case showed no response, though occult KRAS mutation was identified by liquid biopsy explaining resistance

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical benefit demonstrated in the single published case. Theoretical rationale exists (63% EGFR overexpression, low KRAS mutation rate of 8%), but no efficacy data support survival benefit. May work in strictly RAS/BRAF wild-type ITAC

accessibility.rationale: Cetuximab is FDA-approved for colorectal cancer and HNSCC. Widely available. Off-label use for ITAC feasible with appropriate molecular testing

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile: acneiform rash (80%), infusion reactions, hypomagnesemia, diarrhea. Generally manageable. Skin rash correlates with efficacy in other tumor types

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires RAS/BRAF wild-type status (analogous to colorectal cancer). KRAS mutations in 8-10% of ITAC (lower than CRC) and HRAS in 16%. This patient lacks EGFR mutation but may have EGFR overexpression — IHC testing needed. Must rule out occult RAS mutations with sensitive assays (BEAMing)

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody that blocks ligand binding to EGFR, inhibiting downstream RAS-RAF-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling. Also induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). In ITAC with intestinal biology, parallels colorectal cancer where cetuximab is effective in RAS/BRAF wild-type tumors. Requires comprehensive molecular testing including sensitive assays for RAS mutations.

key_evidence.study_name: Nasoethmoidal Intestinal-Type Adenocarcinoma Treated with Cetuximab: Role of Liquid Biopsy and BEAMing

biomarker_requirements: Extended RAS wild-type (KRAS/NRAS exons 2-4) — use sensitive assays such as BEAMing
BRAF V600E wild-type
EGFR overexpression by IHC (63% of ITAC positive)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for CRC and HNSCC — off-label for ITAC

treatments.name: NGS-Guided Basket Trial Enrollment (Tumor-Agnostic Targeted Therapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple basket trials accepting any solid tumor with specific molecular alterations. NCI-MATCH, TAPUR, and institutional basket trials provide access to targeted agents based on NGS results. No ITAC-specific data but tumor-agnostic FDA approvals establish precedent (pembrolizumab for MSI-high, larotrectinib for NTRK fusions)

survival_benefit.rationale: Variable depending on specific molecular alteration identified. Tumor-agnostic responses have been meaningful in other rare cancers. ITAC molecular profiling reveals multiple potentially actionable alterations (MET 64%, PIK3CA 10-22%, BRCA 8-14%, ATM 8-16%)

accessibility.rationale: Requires comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS panel) and identification of actionable alteration. Clinical trials available at academic medical centers. May require travel to trial sites

safety_profile.rationale: Varies by specific agent assigned. Generally targeted therapies are better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy. Trial protocols include safety monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: By definition, treatment matched to specific molecular alteration. ITAC has high rate of potentially actionable alterations across 4 key pathways. NGS profiling strongly recommended for this patient to identify all therapeutic opportunities

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Comprehensive genomic profiling by NGS identifies actionable molecular alterations that can be matched to targeted agents through basket trial enrollment. ITAC frequently harbors alterations in Wnt/beta-catenin, DNA damage response (BRCA1/2, ATM), MAPK, and PI3K pathways. Tumor-agnostic clinical trials allow access to matched therapies regardless of primary tumor histology, leveraging the principle that molecular alterations may predict response independent of tissue of origin.

key_evidence.study_name: Next-generation sequencing for identification of actionable gene mutations in intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma

biomarker_requirements: Comprehensive NGS panel required
Specific actionable alteration must be identified
Common ITAC targets: MET (64%), PIK3CA (10-22%), BRCA1/2 (8-14%), ATM (8-16%), KRAS (8%), HRAS (16%)
notable_side_effects: Variable depending on specific targeted agent
Generally better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial — NCI-MATCH, TAPUR, and institutional basket trials at major academic centers

treatments.name: MET Inhibitor (Capmatinib/Tepotinib) for MET-Altered ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: No clinical data in ITAC. MET-activating mutations found in up to 64% of ITAC (PMC 2022 review). Capmatinib and tepotinib FDA-approved for MET exon 14 skipping NSCLC with strong efficacy (ORR 56-68%). Purely theoretical application to ITAC

survival_benefit.rationale: In MET-altered NSCLC: capmatinib ORR 68%, PFS 12.4 months (treatment-naive). If ITAC MET alterations are functionally analogous, meaningful benefit possible. However, ITAC MET alterations may differ mechanistically from NSCLC MET exon 14 skipping

accessibility.rationale: Capmatinib and tepotinib FDA-approved for NSCLC only. Off-label use for ITAC would require MET mutation/amplification confirmation by NGS and compassionate use or clinical trial access

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated in NSCLC trials. Main toxicities: peripheral edema, nausea, fatigue, vomiting. Grade 3+ events relatively uncommon. Manageable safety profile

biomarker_match.rationale: MET-activating mutations present in up to 64% of ITAC. Requires NGS confirmation of specific MET alteration type (exon 14 skipping, amplification, or activating point mutation). Not all MET alterations may predict response to MET inhibitors

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Capmatinib and tepotinib are selective MET receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitors that block HGF/MET signaling, inhibiting downstream PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK pathways. MET alterations (mutations, amplifications) drive tumor proliferation and survival in up to 64% of ITAC. MET pathway inhibition may have particular relevance given that MET is one of the most frequently altered oncogenes in ITAC molecular profiling studies.

key_evidence.study_name: GEOMETRY mono-1 (capmatinib in MET-altered NSCLC) — applied rationale from ITAC molecular profiling
key_evidence.journal: New England Journal of Medicine / Molecular Basis review PMC

biomarker_requirements: MET exon 14 skipping mutation or MET amplification confirmed by NGS
Up to 64% of ITAC harbor MET-activating alterations
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for MET exon 14 NSCLC — off-label/compassionate use for ITAC

treatments.name: PARP Inhibitor (Olaparib) for BRCA/ATM-Mutated ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: No clinical data in ITAC. BRCA1/2 mutations (8-14%) and ATM alterations (8-16%) identified in ITAC molecular profiling. PARP inhibitors FDA-approved tumor-agnostically for BRCA-mutated cancers. Belgian Phase II study showed olaparib activity in various BRCA/ATM-mutated solid tumors

survival_benefit.rationale: In BRCA-mutated breast/ovarian/pancreatic cancer, olaparib shows significant PFS benefit (HR 0.3-0.6). If ITAC BRCA/ATM alterations confer similar synthetic lethality, meaningful benefit expected. However, no ITAC-specific efficacy data exists

accessibility.rationale: Olaparib FDA-approved for BRCA-mutated breast, ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancers. Tumor-agnostic approval pathway exists. Off-label for ITAC requires confirmed BRCA/ATM mutation by NGS

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity: anemia (most common grade 3+), nausea, fatigue, vomiting. Risk of MDS/AML (1-2% long-term). Oral administration with manageable side effect profile

biomarker_match.rationale: BRCA1/2 mutations in 8-14% and ATM alterations in 8-16% of ITAC. Requires comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS). Only applicable to the biomarker-selected subset. DDR pathway alterations collectively present in a meaningful minority of ITAC

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Olaparib inhibits poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP), trapping PARP on DNA at sites of single-strand breaks. In cells with homologous recombination deficiency (BRCA1/2 or ATM mutations), this leads to unrepaired DNA double-strand breaks and synthetic lethality-mediated cell death. ITAC molecular profiling reveals DNA damage response pathway alterations (BRCA1/2: 8-14%, ATM: 8-16%) that may confer PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

key_evidence.study_name: Efficacy of olaparib in advanced cancers with germline or somatic mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2 and ATM (Belgian Precision Phase II)

biomarker_requirements: Germline or somatic BRCA1/2 mutation
ATM loss-of-function mutation
Comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS) required
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for BRCA-mutated cancers — off-label/compassionate use for ITAC with confirmed mutation

treatments.name: Toripalimab + Carboplatin/Docetaxel (Perioperative Chemo-Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II clinical trial initiated June 2025 (Coherus Biosciences) evaluating toripalimab in combination with carboplatin/docetaxel for sinonasal cancers. No results available yet. Represents first dedicated perioperative chemo-immunotherapy trial for sinonasal tumors

survival_benefit.rationale: No efficacy data available yet. Rationale from perioperative immunotherapy success in other HNSCC subtypes (KEYNOTE-689). Expected modest benefit in ITAC given low immunogenicity, though combination with chemotherapy may enhance response

accessibility.rationale: Currently available only through active clinical trial enrollment. Toripalimab (Loqtorzi) is FDA-approved for nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Limited trial sites

safety_profile.rationale: Expected immune-related adverse events from toripalimab plus standard chemotherapy toxicity (myelosuppression, neuropathy from docetaxel). Combined toxicity profile may be more significant than either alone

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-selected. ITAC low immunogenicity (PD-L1 expression 17%, MSI-high 2%) suggests limited checkpoint inhibitor benefit. Inclusion of chemotherapy backbone may provide independent activity via intestinal biology

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Toripalimab is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody combined with carboplatin (DNA crosslinker) and docetaxel (microtubule stabilizer). Perioperative administration aims to achieve immune priming before surgery and sustained immune surveillance post-operatively. Chemotherapy may enhance immunogenicity through immunogenic cell death and neoantigen release.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Perioperative Toripalimab + Carboplatin/Docetaxel in Sinonasal Cancers (Coherus Biosciences)

treatments.name: Alpelisib (PI3K Inhibitor) for PIK3CA-Mutated ITAC

evidence_level.rationale: No clinical data in ITAC. PIK3CA mutations found in 10-22% of ITAC. Alpelisib FDA-approved for PIK3CA-mutated HR+/HER2- breast cancer. PI3K pathway is one of four key altered pathways in ITAC molecular profiling

survival_benefit.rationale: In PIK3CA-mutated breast cancer (SOLAR-1): PFS 11.0 vs 5.7 months, HR 0.65. Unknown whether similar benefit translates to ITAC. PI3K pathway activation may contribute to ITAC progression but no direct evidence

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for breast cancer only. Off-label use for ITAC requires PIK3CA mutation confirmation and would likely need compassionate use justification. Oral administration

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity: hyperglycemia (requiring glucose monitoring and metformin co-administration), rash, diarrhea, nausea. Grade 3+ hyperglycemia in 37% of breast cancer patients. Requires careful metabolic monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: PIK3CA mutations in 10-22% of ITAC. Requires NGS confirmation. Only applicable to biomarker-selected subset. PI3K pathway is one of four key pathways altered in ITAC

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Alpelisib is a selective PI3Kalpha inhibitor that blocks PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway activation driven by PIK3CA mutations. In ITAC, PI3K pathway alterations represent one of four key oncogenic pathways (alongside Wnt/beta-catenin, MAPK, and DNA damage response). Inhibition of PI3K signaling reduces cell proliferation, survival, and metabolism in PIK3CA-mutated tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: SOLAR-1 (alpelisib in PIK3CA-mutated breast cancer) — rationale applied from ITAC molecular profiling
key_evidence.journal: New England Journal of Medicine / PMC molecular review

biomarker_requirements: PIK3CA activating mutation confirmed by NGS
10-22% of ITAC harbor PIK3CA mutations
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for PIK3CA-mutated breast cancer — off-label/compassionate use for ITAC

clinical_trials.title: Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: No results available yet. Evaluating pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as neoadjuvant treatment in locally advanced sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (SNUC) and potentially other sinonasal histologies.

clinical_trials.title: Perioperative Toripalimab + Carboplatin/Docetaxel in Sinonasal Cancers
clinical_trials.early_results: No results available. First dedicated perioperative chemo-immunotherapy trial for sinonasal cancers. Evaluates anti-PD-1 (toripalimab) combined with platinum-taxane doublet.

clinical_trials.title: Assessment of Concomitant Cisplatin Benefit in Sinonasal Tumors with Adjuvant Radiotherapy
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized trial evaluating whether addition of concurrent cisplatin to adjuvant radiotherapy improves outcomes in sinonasal cancers. Includes ITAC histology. Results pending.

clinical_trials.title: Protons vs Photons for Sinonasal Cancers (British Randomized Trial)
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized comparison of proton therapy versus photon IMRT for sinonasal malignancies including ITAC. First randomized proton vs photon trial in this disease site. Results pending.

clinical_trials.title: Molecular Analysis for Therapy Choice / Targeted Agent and Profiling Utilization Registry
biomarker_requirements: Actionable molecular alteration by NGS
Relevant for ITAC: MET alterations (64%), PIK3CA (10-22%), BRCA1/2 (8-14%), ATM (8-16%)
clinical_trials.early_results: Tumor-agnostic basket trials matching patients to targeted therapies based on molecular profiling. Multiple arms available for common ITAC alterations. Accept rare tumor histologies including sinonasal adenocarcinoma.

clinical_trials.title: De-escalation of Radiotherapy in Sinonasal Cancers (Photons or Protons)
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating whether radiation dose de-escalation is feasible in select sinonasal cancers while maintaining oncologic outcomes. May include ITAC histology.

combination_strategies.rationale: Surgery addresses macroscopic disease. IMRT targets microscopic margins and regional at-risk volume. Topical 5-FU directly treats the surgical cavity surface where 88% of recurrences originate locally. Triple-modality addresses the 34-50% local recurrence rate. 5-year DFS 86% with surgery + topical 5-FU in woodworkers' adenocarcinoma series.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Surgery + Adjuvant IMRT or Proton Therapy
combination_strategies.rationale: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy exploits intestinal biology (CDX2+/CK20+) for tumor downstaging before surgery. 40% pCR rate overall (83% in TP53 wild-type). May enable less extensive resection. Sequential multimodality approach maximizes local control. Consider TP53 testing to select patients most likely to benefit.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Extrapolated from colorectal cancer — no ITAC data
combination_strategies.rationale: Mirrors established colorectal cancer paradigm: fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy combined with anti-EGFR therapy in RAS/BRAF wild-type tumors. ITAC shares intestinal immunophenotype (CDX2+, CK20+, villin+) with colorectal cancer. KRAS mutation rate lower in ITAC (8%) than CRC (40%), suggesting more patients may be eligible. Requires sensitive RAS testing (BEAMing) given occult mutation detection in case reports.

Concurrent cisplatin radiosensitizes tumor cells during adjuvant radiation. Standard approach in HNSCC showing survival benefit. SANTAL trial specifically evaluates this in sinonasal cancers. May be particularly beneficial for T3 tumors with cribriform plate involvement where local control is critical.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Retrospective series — 70% of proton patients received concurrent systemic therapy
combination_strategies.rationale: Proton therapy allows dose escalation (73.6 GyRBE) while reducing normal tissue toxicity. Concurrent chemotherapy provides radiosensitization. 5-year local control 80% in mixed sinonasal cohort. Particularly advantageous for T3 cribriform plate tumors where optic nerve and brain proximity limits photon dose.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple studies demonstrate high-volume nasal saline irrigation reduces crusting, infection, and improves quality of life after endoscopic sinonasal surgery and radiation therapy. Standard recommendation in all sinonasal surgery post-operative protocols.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces nasal crusting, prevents infection, maintains nasal hygiene, improves mucociliary clearance, and enhances quality of life. Particularly important given extensive mucosal disruption from endoscopic resection and radiation therapy in the ethmoid/skull base region.

supportive_care.approach: Occupational Health Surveillance and Hardwood Dust Exposure Cessation
supportive_care.evidence: IARC classifies wood dust as Group 1 carcinogen. Odds ratio for sinonasal adenocarcinoma with wood dust exposure is 10.28 (95% CI: 5.92-17.85). Both duration and average level of exposure independently contribute to risk. Engineering controls (exhaust ventilation, dust collection) and personal protective equipment (respirators) reduce exposure.
supportive_care.benefit: Elimination of ongoing carcinogenic exposure reduces risk of second primary sinonasal malignancy and may reduce recurrence risk. Occupational health assessment and workplace modification are essential components of survivorship care for this furniture maker.

supportive_care.approach: Multidisciplinary Skull Base Rehabilitation (Speech, Swallowing, Vision Monitoring)
supportive_care.evidence: Skull base and sinonasal neoplasm treatment threatens basic functions including vision, olfaction, speech, and swallowing. Interdisciplinary supportive care lessens functional impairments. Post-operative and post-RT monitoring of optic nerve function is critical given cribriform plate involvement proximity to optic structures.
supportive_care.benefit: Preserves or restores speech and swallowing function, monitors for radiation-related optic neuropathy, addresses anosmia/hyposmia, and provides psychological support for facial/functional changes. Regular ophthalmologic examinations recommended given radiation dose proximity to optic nerves.

supportive_care.evidence: Local recurrence rate 34% with over 10% recurring after 10 years (Frontiers in Oncology 2025, 200-patient series). Half of recurrences detected within 2 years but late recurrences are characteristic of ITAC. Endoscopic surveillance with nasal endoscopy and MRI is standard practice.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of local recurrence enables less extensive salvage surgery and improved outcomes. Recommended schedule: every 3 months for years 1-2, every 6 months for years 3-5, then annually indefinitely given the risk of very late recurrence unique to ITAC.

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Support and Exercise Program During Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: Head and neck cancer patients undergoing chemoradiation experience significant weight loss, mucositis, and dysphagia. Nutritional counseling and exercise programs during treatment improve treatment tolerance, reduce treatment breaks, and may improve survival outcomes based on head and neck cancer meta-analyses.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains nutritional status during treatment, reduces treatment-related weight loss, improves treatment completion rates, and enhances quality of life. Prehabilitation before surgery may improve post-operative recovery.

supportive_care.approach: Psychosocial Support and Occupational Cancer Support Services
supportive_care.evidence: ITAC as an occupational cancer in woodworkers carries distinct psychosocial implications including workplace exposure guilt, occupational disability, and potential workers' compensation considerations. Head and neck cancer patients have elevated rates of depression and anxiety.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses psychological impact of occupational cancer diagnosis, assists with workplace accommodations or disability applications, connects to workers' compensation resources, and provides counseling for anxiety/depression. Especially important for a 45-year-old working-age patient.
